description: A substance or surface is 'shumeko' when it clings to another surface after contact and does not separate from it easily. Honey can be 'shumeko' on a spoon, and uncured repair paste can be 'shumeko' before a joint is complete. 'wirua' (wet) reports liquid on or within a surface; 'lorea' (connect) reports the relation made between pieces.
articulatory_notes: Raise the tongue blade toward the postalveolar region for voiceless 'sh' and let air pass through the narrow channel into rounded high-back 'u'. Close the lips for voiced nasal 'm', carry the voice through the nose, then open into stressed true-mid front 'e'. Raise the back of the tongue against the soft palate for 'k' and release into rounded true-mid back 'o'.
sound_symbolism: The sustained opening fricative gives way to firm contact at the lips and then the soft palate. In Phi, that interrupted path offers a tactile mnemonic for material that catches on contact instead of releasing at once.
usage_notes: The quality-noun rule supplies stickiness or adhesion. A change of state is 'shumeko kelu' (become sticky), and causative 'ka' can introduce what caused the change.

semantic_domains.physical: a material or surface that adheres on contact and resists easy separation
